data.text/markdown:
Quantum Spin in Geometric Algebra

In the GA approach to quantum mechanics, spin states are rotors — even-grade elements of Cl(3,0) — not column vectors in a complex Hilbert space. The key insight: every 2×2 unitary matrix (SU(2)) corresponds to a rotor in Cl(3,0). The Pauli matrices become bivectors, and the Bloch sphere is just the space of rotations of a reference direction.
Conventional QM Geometric Algebra
Spin state ||(\vert\psi\rangle||) Rotor ||(\psi||) (even multivector)
Pauli matrix ||(\sigma_k||) Basis vector ||(e_k||)
Spin operator ||(\hat{S}_k = \frac{\hbar}{2}\sigma_k||) Bivector ||(\frac{\hbar}{2}Ie_k||)
Expectation ||(\langle\psi\vert\sigma_k\vert\psi\rangle||) Scalar ||((\psi\, e_3\, \tilde\psi) \cdot e_k||)
Time evolution ||(e^{-iHt/\hbar}||) Rotor ||(e^{-Bt}||)

The Algebra of Spin

We work in Cl(3,0) — three Euclidean basis vectors. The pseudoscalar ||(I = e_1 e_2 e_3||) plays the role of the imaginary unit ||(i||) (it squares to ||(-1||) and commutes with even elements). The three bivectors ||(e_{12}||), ||(e_{23}||), ||(e_{31}||) each square to ||(-1||) and form a Lie algebra under the commutator — isomorphic to ||(\mathfrak{su}(2)||).

Bivector basis (each squares to ||(-1||), like ||(i||)):
  • ||(e_{12}||), ||(\quad e_{12}^2 = -1||)
  • ||(e_{23}||), ||(\quad e_{23}^2 = -1||)
  • ||(-e_{13}||), ||(\quad e_{31}^2 = -1||)
Commutation relations — bivectors form a Lie algebra (isomorphic to ||(\mathfrak{su}(2)||)):
  • ||([e_{12},\, e_{23}] = e_{13}||)
  • ||([e_{23},\, e_{31}] = -e_{12}||)
  • ||([e_{31},\, e_{12}] = -e_{23}||)
Pseudoscalar ||(I||), ||(\quad I^2 = -1||) ||(I||) commutes with all even elements and plays the role of ||(i||) in the Pauli algebra.

Spin States as Rotors

A spin state ||(\psi||) is a rotor — an even-grade element with ||(\psi\tilde\psi = 1||). The spin vector (Bloch vector) is ||(\mathbf{s} = \psi\, e_3\, \tilde\psi||): the image of the reference direction ||(e_3||) under the rotation ||(\psi||). This is the direction the spin "points".
State Rotor ||(\psi||) Spin vector ||(\mathbf{s}||)
Spin-up (||(+z||)) ||(1||) ||(e_3||)
Spin-down (||(-z||)) ||(e_1 e_3||) ||(-e_3||)
Spin ||(+x||) ||(\cos 45° + \sin 45°\, e_3 e_1||) ||(e_1||)
Spin ||(+y||) ||(\cos 45° + \sin 45°\, e_3 e_2||) ||(e_2||)

State Rotor ||(\psi||) Spin vector ||((s_x, s_y, s_z)||)
Spin ↑z ||(1||) (+0, +0, +1)
Spin ↓z ||(e_{13}||) (+0, +0, -1)
Spin +x ||(0.707107 + 0.707107 e_{13}||) (+1, +0, +0)
Spin +y ||(0.707107 + 0.707107 e_{23}||) (+0, +1, +0)
Spin −x ||(0.707107 - 0.707107 e_{13}||) (-1, +0, +0)

The Bloch Sphere

Every pure spin state maps to a point on the unit sphere via its spin vector. The rotor ||(\psi(\theta, \varphi) = R_\varphi\, R_\theta||) tilts ||(e_3||) to the direction ||((\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\; \sin\theta\sin\varphi,\; \cos\theta)||). Drag the sliders to move the spin state on the Bloch sphere.

data.text/markdown:
Measurement Probabilities

For a spin state with spin vector ||(\mathbf{s}||), the probability of measuring spin-up along direction ||(\hat{n}||) is: ||[P(+\hat{n}) = \frac{1 + \hat{n} \cdot \mathbf{s}}{2} = \cos^2\!\frac{\alpha}{2}||]where ||(\alpha||) is the angle between ||(\mathbf{s}||) and ||(\hat{n}||). This is the Malus's law of quantum spin — identical to the classical law for polarized light.

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='Hstk-0' random-id='9610cc9d-5a29-6d88-ebd5-5f2127c3f199'><marimo-slider data-initial-value='60' data-label='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">State θ (tilt from z)</span></span>"' data-start='0' data-stop='180' data-step='1' data-steps='[]' data-debounce='false' data-disabled='false' data-orientation='"horizontal"' data-show-value='false' data-include-input='false' data-full-width='false'></marimo-slider></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown:
Sequential Stern–Gerlach

A classic thought experiment: pass a beam through three Stern–Gerlach devices.
  1. First device (along ||(z||)): select spin-up → state ||(|\!\uparrow_z\rangle||)
  2. Second device (at angle ||(\alpha||)): select spin-up → state ||(|\!\uparrow_\alpha\rangle||)
  3. Third device (along ||(z||)): measure spin-up
The total transmission probability is ||(\cos^2(\alpha/2) \times \cos^2(\alpha/2) = \cos^4(\alpha/2)||). At ||(\alpha = 90°||) this is ||(1/4||) — the intermediate measurement "restores" some spin-up that the first device would have blocked completely.

<li><marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(\alpha = 0°||)</marimo-tex></li>
<li><marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(P||)</marimo-tex>(pass 2nd) <marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(= \cos^2(\alpha/2) = 1.0000||)</marimo-tex></li>
<li><marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(P||)</marimo-tex>(pass 3rd | passed 2nd) <marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(= \cos^2(\alpha/2) = 1.0000||)</marimo-tex></li>
<li><strong>Total: <marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(P = 1.0000||)</marimo-tex></strong></li>
</ul></span></div>

data.text/markdown:
Larmor Precession

A spin in a magnetic field ||(\mathbf{B} = B\,e_3||) precesses about the field axis. The Hamiltonian bivector is ||(\Omega = -\frac{\omega}{2}\,e_1 e_2||) (where ||(\omega = \gamma B||) is the Larmor frequency). Time evolution is a rotor: ||[U(t) = e^{-\Omega t} = e^{\omega t/2\; e_1 e_2}||]This is just a rotation about ||(e_3||) — the spin vector traces a cone, with ||(s_z||) constant.

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='TqIu-0' random-id='ca3384fb-21d7-85e4-9550-24cb32fee1e5'><marimo-slider data-initial-value='60' data-label='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">Initial tilt θ (degrees)</span></span>"' data-start='1' data-stop='90' data-step='1' data-steps='[]' data-debounce='false' data-disabled='false' data-orientation='"horizontal"' data-show-value='false' data-include-input='false' data-full-width='false'></marimo-slider></marimo-ui-element>

<div style='display: flex;flex: 1;flex-direction: column;justify-content: flex-start;align-items: normal;flex-wrap: nowrap;gap: 0.5rem'><marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"application/vnd.marimo+mimebundle"' data-data='"{\"image/png\": \"data:image/png;base64,\", \"__metadata__\": {\"image/png\": {\"width\": 1098, \"height\": 437}}}"'></marimo-mime-renderer><span class="markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents"><span class="paragraph"><marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(s_z = \cos\theta = 0.8090||)</marimo-tex> (constant — precession preserves <marimo-tex class="arithmatex">||(z||)</marimo-tex>-component)</span></span></div>

data.text/markdown:
Spin Rotations and SU(2) Double Cover

A ||(360°||) rotation returns a vector to itself, but the rotor picks up a sign: ||(\psi \to -\psi||). You need ||(720°||) to get back to ||(+\psi||). This is the famous spinor sign flip — the geometric origin of fermion statistics.

Angle Scalar part Rotor ||(\psi||) ||(s_z||)
0° +1.0000 ||(1||) +1.0000
90° +0.7071 ||(0.707107 - 0.707107 e_{12}||) +1.0000
180° +0.0000 ||(-e_{12}||) +1.0000
270° -0.7071 ||(-0.707107 - 0.707107 e_{12}||) +1.0000
360° -1.0000 ||(-1||) +1.0000
450° -0.7071 ||(-0.707107 + 0.707107 e_{12}||) +1.0000
540° -0.0000 ||(e_{12}||) +1.0000
630° +0.7071 ||(0.707107 + 0.707107 e_{12}||) +1.0000
720° +1.0000 ||(1||) +1.0000
At 360°: the rotor is ||(-1||) (not ||(+1||)!). The spin vector returns to ||(e_3||), but the rotor has flipped sign. Only at 720° does ||(\psi||) return to ||(+1||).

Rotor Interpolation (SLERP)

Because spin states are rotors, interpolation is natural: scale the bivector in the exponential. ||[\psi(t) = \exp\!\big(t \cdot \log\psi_1\big), \qquad t \in [0, 1]||]This traces a geodesic on the Bloch sphere — the shortest path between two spin states.

data.text/markdown:
Symbolic Identities

The symbolic layer verifies algebraic identities as expression trees.

• Double reverse: ~~ψ ||(\;\to\;||) ||(ψ||)
  • Rotor normalization: ψ~ψ ||(\;\to\;||) ||(1||)
  • Sandwich grade: ⟨ψ e₃ ~ψ⟩₁ ||(\;\to\;||) ||(\langle ψ e₃ \tilde{ψ} \rangle_{1}||)
  • Norm of unit: ‖unit(e₃)‖ ||(\;\to\;||) ||(\lVert n̂ \rVert||)
